quotes.verbatim_quote: Try building diatonic 7th chords from the following forms:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core instructional directive for practicing chord scale construction.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Remember to go down in all your chord scales, too (that is: E* D#¢7 C#m7 B7 etc.)
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explicit rule that chord scales must be practiced descending as well as ascending.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Examples of some decoration: m und these chords up softens

the dissonance.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Method's only explicit guidance on how chord voicing decoration reduces dissonance.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
1. PROGRESSIONS BUILT FROM SCALEWISE ROOT MOVEMENTS (Major Key) (will be abbreviated: SWR)

Another name for these progressions might be “Progressions derived from diatonic chord scales”. Analyze the
examples given and you will see why.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines SWR and links it to diatonic chord scales.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
2. PROGRESSIONS BUILT FROM SCALEWISE BASS LINES (Major Key) (will be abbreviated: SWB)

Many pieces of music have been written using these kinds of sounds and you will probably find them to be very
attractive. The logic behind them will be clear if you understand your fundamentals of music theory concerning
inversions. If you don’t, enjoy them anyway, but if you want to construct similar variations, you may have to get
your music theory act together.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines SWB and requires inversion knowledge as prerequisite.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Also, Volume 2 will go inte this subject of ‘Bass Progressions” very thoroughly. The few brief examples presented
here are just “‘teasers’’.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Scope note signaling brevity of SWB treatment here.
